skill.name: mermaid-diagrams
skill.description: Create clear software diagrams with Mermaid syntax for architecture, workflows, data models, class structures, and sequence flows.
skill.summary: Create Mermaid diagrams for software architecture, workflows, databases, classes, and API flows.
skill.author: softaworks

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, and blocker patterns, but review shows they are overwhelmingly Mermaid documentation syntax and visible examples. The only confirmed risks are optional rendering commands, external links, a Docker volume example, and one authentication diagram that mentions localStorage token storage. No prompt injection, hidden execution, credential exfiltration, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Mermaid Syntax Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: Most static command-execution alerts are false positives caused by fenced Mermaid examples and diagram arrows. The reviewed lines are documentation snippets, not executable Ruby, shell, or JavaScript code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are fenced Mermaid blocks or Mermaid arrow syntax. I found no executable source file or automatic command invocation tied to these examples.

low_findings.title: Optional Rendering Commands Require User Execution
low_findings.description: The skill documents Mermaid CLI and Docker export commands. They are not executed by the skill, but users who run them may install external packages, pull a container image, or mount the current directory.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are real CLI and Docker examples, but they appear only in documentation. There is no evidence that the skill runs them automatically.

low_findings.title: External Links and CDN Import Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The network alerts point to Mermaid documentation links, example clickable diagram URLs, and an optional CDN import snippet. These references do not collect data or run without explicit user action.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation and Mermaid examples. I found no hidden network request, credential submission, or obfuscated endpoint.

low_findings.title: Authentication Diagram Shows Browser Token Storage
low_findings.description: One sequence diagram example includes a frontend step that stores a JWT token in localStorage. This is not executable code, but it can normalize a risky web-authentication storage pattern without warning.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is clearly part of a diagram example, so direct exploitability is low. The security concern is educational guidance rather than runtime behavior.

content.value_statement: Technical teams need diagrams that stay close to code and are easy to review. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code produce Mermaid diagrams for architecture, workflows, schemas, and interactions.
actual_capabilities: Selects suitable Mermaid diagram types for architecture, process, data, and interaction documentation.
Creates class diagrams, sequence diagrams, flowcharts, ERDs, C4 diagrams, state diagrams, git graphs, Gantt charts, and charts.
Applies Mermaid syntax for relationships, participants, entities, cardinality, subgraphs, notes, and styling.
Provides guidance for embedding diagrams in Markdown, GitHub, GitLab, VS Code, Notion, Obsidian, and Confluence.
Explains export options using Mermaid Live Editor, Mermaid CLI, or Docker when users choose to render images.
limitations: It provides diagram guidance and text output; it does not render images by itself.
Users must validate Mermaid syntax in their target renderer because feature support can differ by platform.
Advanced layout features such as ELK may require extra renderer configuration.
Export commands and external editors require user-controlled tools, network access, or local installation.

use_cases.title: Document Service Architecture
use_cases.description: Create C4 or flowchart diagrams that show systems, containers, services, databases, and external dependencies.

use_cases.title: Explain API and Product Flows
use_cases.description: Create sequence diagrams and flowcharts for login, checkout, onboarding, approval, and error-handling paths.

use_cases.title: Model Data and Domains
use_cases.description: Create ERDs and class diagrams that define entities, attributes, keys, methods, relationships, and ownership rules.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Flowchart
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for mapping a simple workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Mermaid flowchart for this process: [describe the steps]. Use clear labels and include decision points.

prompt_templates.title: Document an API Sequence
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for request and response flows.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Mermaid sequence diagram for this API flow: [describe actors, services, success path, and error paths]. Include participants and alt blocks.

prompt_templates.title: Model a Database Schema
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for relational data modeling.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Mermaid ERD for these tables: [list tables, columns, keys, and relationships]. Show cardinality and important constraints.

prompt_templates.title: Create a C4 Architecture View
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for system design documentation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create Mermaid C4 diagrams for this system: [describe users, containers, services, databases, and external systems]. Provide context and container views.

output_examples.input: A team asks for a checkout process diagram with payment success, payment failure, inventory checks, and confirmation email.
output_examples.output: A focused flowchart that starts at cart review, branches through payment and inventory decisions, and ends with confirmation or recovery paths.

output_examples.input: A backend developer asks for an ERD for users, orders, products, and line items.
output_examples.output: A database relationship diagram that names each entity, shows primary and foreign keys, and marks one-to-many relationships clearly.

output_examples.input: An architect asks for a C4 view of a web app, API, database, payment provider, and email provider.
output_examples.output: A system context and container view that separates internal containers from external systems and labels communication protocols.

best_practices: Start with one clear purpose per diagram, then add detail only where it improves understanding.
Use labels that match code, database, or service names so diagrams remain easy to maintain.
Validate diagrams in the same renderer used by the final documentation platform.
anti_patterns: Do not combine every system, class, and workflow into one oversized diagram.
Do not use generic node names when real domain names are available.
Do not run export commands or use online editors with sensitive content unless your environment allows it.

faq.question: Does this skill render Mermaid diagrams?
faq.answer: No. It helps create Mermaid text and explains how to render it with tools such as GitHub, Mermaid Live, or Mermaid CLI.

faq.question: Which diagram types does it support?
faq.answer: It covers class diagrams, sequence diagrams, flowcharts, ERDs, C4 diagrams, state diagrams, git graphs, Gantt charts, and charts.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is it safe for private architecture documentation?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation-only, but avoid sending sensitive diagrams to online editors or external rendering services.

faq.question: Why did security scanning flag many issues?
faq.answer: The scanner misread Mermaid arrows, authentication labels, links, and examples as executable or network behavior.

faq.question: When should I choose a C4 diagram instead of a flowchart?
faq.answer: Use C4 diagrams for architecture structure. Use flowcharts for process steps, decisions, and user journeys.
